name: The Oneiroi (Dreams)
shortDescription: The Oneiroi are the personifications of dreams in Greek mythology. They were children of Nyx (Night) and were responsible for bringing dreams to mortals and sometimes gods.
description: The Oneiroi, in Greek mythology, are the personifications of dreams. They were the offspring of Nyx (Night), often without a father, though some accounts attribute their parentage to Erebus (Darkness). They resided in the Underworld, near the cave of Hypnos (Sleep), and emerged from twin gates – one of horn, through which true dreams passed, and one of ivory, through which false dreams emerged. The Oneiroi were messengers, delivering dreams to mortals, often at the behest of the gods. Morpheus was their leader and was skilled in taking human form. Other prominent Oneiroi included Ikelos (or Phobetor), who brought dreams of animals, and Phantasos, who brought dreams of inanimate objects. Their roles were diverse, ranging from delivering comforting visions to instilling terror and guiding mortals. The Oneiroi held a significant position in ancient Greek culture, as dreams were believed to be pathways to divine communication, prophecy, and insight into the future or the subconscious mind. Their connection to Nyx reinforces their association with the night, mystery, and the hidden realms of existence. They were important in both personal interpretation of dreams and in understanding the will of the gods.
